semantic.description: Given a bounded function \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \), a partition \( P \) of \( [a,b] \) is a finite collection of points \( P=\{x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n\} \) with \( a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n=b \), dividing the interval into subintervals \( [x_{i-1},x_i] \).

For a bounded function \( f \) on \( [a,b] \) and a partition \( P \), let \( M_i=\sup_{x\in[x_{i-1},x_i]} f(x) \) and \( m_i=\inf_{x\in[x_{i-1},x_i]} f(x) \). The Darboux upper sum is \( U(f,P)=\sum_{i=1}^n M_i(x_i-x_{i-1}) \) and the Darboux lower sum is \( L(f,P)=\sum_{i=1}^n m_i(x_i-x_{i-1}) \).

The upper integral of a bounded function \( f \) on \( [a,b] \) is \( U(f)=\inf_{P} U(f,P) \) and the lower integral is \( L(f)=\sup_{P} L(f,P) \), where the infimum and supremum range over all partitions \( P \) of \( [a,b] \). The inequality \( L(f) \leq U(f) \) always holds.

semantic.statement: A bounded function \( f \) is Riemann integrable on \( [a,b] \) if and only if its upper and lower integrals coincide, that is \( U(f) = L(f) \). In that case their common value equals the integral \( \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \).

A bounded function \( f \) is Riemann integrable on \( [a,b] \) if and only if for every \( \varepsilon > 0 \) there exists a partition \( P \) of \( [a,b] \) such that \( U(f,P) - L(f,P) < \varepsilon \).

A bounded function \( f \) on \( [a,b] \) is Riemann integrable if it satisfies any one of the following conditions: \( f \) is continuous on \( [a,b] \); \( f \) is monotone on \( [a,b] \); \( f \) has only finitely many discontinuities on \( [a,b] \).

semantic.description: A set \( D \subset [a,b] \) has Lebesgue measure zero if for every \( \varepsilon > 0 \) it can be covered by a countable collection of intervals whose total length is less than \( \varepsilon \). Finite and countable sets, including the rationals, have measure zero.

semantic.statement: A bounded function \( f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R} \) is Riemann integrable if and only if its set of discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero.
